cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why a novelist can expect the publisher to print the manuscript exactly as written. The answer options are (A) through (D). First, I need to refer back to the passage provided. The passage starts by talking about the novel as a medium of the written word, emphasizing that it's consumed individually and is limited to writing as a single channel. Then it mentions that the novelist has absolute control over the text until it's published, and that even established novelists can submit their manuscript expecting it to be printed as written. The key part here is when the passage says, "The novelist keeps absolute control over his text until it is published and received by the audience." It also states that even if an editor suggests revisions, the writer isn't obligated to make them. This points to the idea that the novelist has the final say on the text before publication. Looking at the options: (A) Because the novelist keeps absolute control over his text. This directly quotes the passage, so this seems like a strong candidate. (B) Because the paperback is portable and adaptable. The passage mentions this, but that's more about the convenience of the medium rather than the reason why the publisher would print it as is. So B doesn't directly answer the question. (C) Because the novel is limited to a single channel (writing). This is mentioned in the passage, but that's a characteristic of the medium. It explains why the novel can go anywhere without cost considerations, but not necessarily why the publisher prints it as written. The control aspect is more about author's authority, not the medium's limitations. (D) Because the novelist is seldom advised by editors. The passage actually says the opposite: "He may be advised by his editor to revise his text, but if the writer refused to meet this condition no one would be surprised."
